bio.title: Colonel
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Col. George Stephen Cantlie (1867-1956), D.S.O. of the Black Watch.

notable.notable_blurb: WWI Black Watch commander, 'Dear Wee Celia' war

career.organization: 42nd Battalion (Royal Highlanders of Canada / Black Watch), Canadian Expeditionary Force
career.notes: Original commanding officer of the 42nd Battalion CEF, Royal Highlanders of Canada (Black Watch). Served as commander until the end of 1916.
Organized the 20th Reserve Battalion and commanded it until 1918.
Served with the 5th Royal Scots before WWI.

military_service.unit: 42nd Battalion (Royal Highlanders of Canada / Black Watch), CEF; Distinguished Service Order 1916
military_service.notes: Distinguished Service Order awarded Dec 29, 1916 / Jan 1, 1917 (London Gazette). Service file: B1473-S056. Survived war. Suffered what was then called battle fatigue; sent home 1917.

narrative_blocks.header: Original CO of Canada's Black Watch in WWI
narrative_blocks.body: Lt.-Col. George Stephen Cantlie was the original commanding officer of the 42nd Battalion, Royal Highlanders of Canada (the Black Watch) in the Canadian Expeditionary Force — taking the battalion to France in 1914 and commanding it through the end of 1916. He earned the Distinguished Service Order awarded December 1916. After his battalion command he organized and led the 20th Reserve Battalion until 1918, when battle fatigue sent him home.

narrative_blocks.header: Pressed flowers for baby Celia from the trenches
narrative_blocks.body: During his command across the battlefields of France in 1915 and 1916, Col. Cantlie picked flowers from bivouac fields and pressed them into letters home to his infant daughter Celia — born 1913, only one year old when he shipped out. He wanted her to have something to remember him by should he not return. Over three hundred such letters were preserved by his daughter and granddaughter; ten of them, written during the Battle of the Somme with pressed daisies, poppies, yellow roses, and heather, form the basis of the touring War Flowers exhibition at the Canadian War Museum.
